issues.description: Government control or restriction of advanced AI capabilities through trade, export, or compute access.

issues.display_name: Military and National Security Uses of AI
issues.description: Positions on the use of AI in military, intelligence, or national security contexts.
A candidate's general stance on regulating AI systems.
Positions on AI systems designed for emotional, relational, or social interaction.
Views on protecting minors from harms related to AI or AI-mediated platforms.
Positions on the development, regulation, or public impact of data center infrastructure as it relates to AI, including energy consumption and grid reliability concerns tied to AI workloads.
Positions on AI-driven job displacement, reskilling programs, unemployment policy, and tax policy for distributing AI-driven wealth.
Views on AI-generated misinformation, scam prevention, fraud targeting vulnerable populations, and synthetic media disclosure requirements.
Stance on federal preemption of state AI laws, states' rights to regulate AI, and the patchwork of local vs. national regulatory approaches.
Positions on AI training data rights, copyright protections for creators and publishers, and liability for AI-generated content.

positions.summary: Emphasizes importance of public comment before new data center construction begins.

House Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence chair; joined bipartisan effort to stop AI chip smuggling to China; stated 'the global AI race against China is moving much faster than many think' and 'Export controls on semiconductor manufacturing equipment and AI chips have strengthened America’s advantage in the AI Arms Race.'
Sponsored FY26 Intelligence Authorization Act which encourages development of AI for US intelligence community.
Voiced national security concerns on deepfakes manipulating comments from world leaders.
Talks about the importance of testing and regulating the power of AI on The Contrarian; lectured on the topic "AI: To Innovate or Annihilate."
Described data centers as an important component of a booming 'AI economy' to the Arkansas Democrat Gazette; also talked about data centers causing an increase in utilities and water.
Showed concern on how AI could rapidly replace white-collar jobs in an Arkansas Democrat Gazette article.

As a member of the AI Task Force, Hill endorsed its findings, which included that AI is critical to national security and that Congress should focus on overseeing AI activities for national security and expanding AI training at the Defense Department.

Laid out two personal guiding principles: a "human in the loop" when using AI, and a requirement that firms disclose and document how they use it; sponsored the Unleashing AI Innovation in Financial Services Act to let companies experiment with AI through sandbox programs at federal financial agencies; favors light-touch, sector-specific approaches over broad mandates; said Congress doesn't want to put a rock on top of innovation.

Said proposed data centers offer extensive capital expenditure utility related funding for water, power generation, and grid expansion that exceed forecasted requirements, benefitting the surrounding communities by creating jobs and increasing the tax base.

Hill specifically called out deepfakes as a concern, saying the AI Task Force's report "discusses justifiable concerns about the vulnerabilities faced by Americans when confronted by illicit or fraudulent uses of AI" including "deep fakes, confusing results because of hallucinations and cyber theft."

2020 House Budget Committee hearing: stated 'Congress must ensure actions do not stifle innovation' and 'create regulatory environment that supports - not hinders - private industry' in AI development.
Libertarian candidate promised to 'champion technological innovation and progress' and 'advocate for policies that harness power of AI and emerging technologies' while ensuring 'privacy protections.'
Advocates for AI regulation, with a focus on banning harmful deepfakes, as a core public policy interest.
Emphasizes importance of public comment before new data center construction begins.

Has framed U.S. leadership in AI as a national-security imperative, warning that losing the AI race to China would undermine U.S. defense systems, but has not taken a specific position on autonomous weapons or military AI applications.

Pro-innovation because of the US-China race, but has said that "maybe humankind would've been better off if we didn't discover this"; supports permitting reform and infrastructure acceleration rather than AI-specific regulation.

As House Natural Resources Committee chair, sponsored the bipartisan SPEED Act to streamline NEPA permitting for data centers and energy infrastructure, framing the bill as essential to powering U.S. AI development.

Supports the KIDS (Kids Internet and Digital Safety) Act, arguing that newer online threats including the misuse of AI must be addressed to protect minors, and has separately introduced the Focus on Learning Act on cell phones in schools.

Says growing presence of data centers and AI isn't a bad thing; wants developers to be transparent about plans; speculates that Cotton's DATA Act will lower energy costs; wants state-level regulators to have authority over data centers.

Introduced the Chip Security Act, which would require location verification mechanisms on AI chips subject to export controls and mandate reporting to Commerce's Bureau of Industry and Security if chips are diverted or tampered with; also co-sponsored the America First GAIN AI Act to prioritize American companies' access to AI chips ahead of adversaries.
Introduced legislation that would make it easier for regulators to block large tech companies from buying up rivals or nascent competitors.
Introduced DATA Act allowing AI data centers to bypass federal electricity regulations and build independent energy infrastructure; pushed off-grid power plan for AI data centers.
Wrote a social media post about holding AI chatbot companies accountable.
Cosponsored GUARD Act (S.3062) - Guidelines for User Age-verification and Responsible Dialogue Act of 2025.
Voted to strike the section relating to AI preemption.
26-year Army career including electronic warfare roles; Acting Assistant Secretary of Defense for Cyber Policy; testimony on cyber resilience and AI integration into military operations.
Leading the Future, via an affiliated group called American Mission, spent $500k on Buckhout's campaign.

Co-sponsored Secure Artificial Intelligence Act with Don Beyer to establish AI Security Center and enhance monitoring of AI security/safety incidents; sponsored AI Incident Reporting and Security Enhancement Act (passed House Science Committee); member of bipartisan House Task Force on AI.
Hosted roundtable with Biden administration on AI; emphasized students learning responsible AI use and need for AI research focused on developing AI for good.

Rep. Ross visited Wake Tech Community College to learn more of a National Science Foundation grant to prepare students for "Industry 5.0," focusing on human-centered manufacturing that combines human talent with AI, robotics, and advanced technology.

Led a letter warning an AI moratorium would undermine bipartisan protections for women and girls and prevent states from responding to rapidly evolving AI-enabled threats, specifically on the growing use of generative AI to stalk and track women, violate the privacy of domestic violence survivors, and create non-consensual porn deepfakes.
Led a group of women lawmakers to urge Speaker Mike Johnson and Dem Leader Hakeem Jeffries to reject any effort to include a broad moratorium on state and local AI regulation in the NDAA.

Sent a letter to the FDA Commissioner regarding regulatory frameworks for AI in health care; states that we must be judicious in cultivating a regulatory environment that protects consumers and encourages innovation.
Recommended humans in the loop to identify and remedy potential flaws in civil rights and civil liberties in the Bipartisan House Task Force Report on Artificial Intelligence.

Co-Chair of House Democratic Commission on AI; member of bipartisan House Task Force on AI; criticized White House AI Framework for not putting people first; introduced HBCU Artificial Intelligence Research Leadership Act and Expanding AI Voices Act; member of House Science, Space, and Technology Committee.
Championed priorities including assessing and understanding AI's rising environmental and energy impacts.
Released a report and launched an investigation emphasizing the need for transparency and clarity regarding the impact of AI on American jobs following historic layoffs.

Introduced Protecting Consumers from Deceptive AI Act to establish technical standards and guidelines for generatie Ai content and ensure that the use of this technology is disclosed when it's used to create or modify audio/visual content; wrote a letter warning an AI moratorium would undermine bipartisan protections for women and girls and prevent states from responding to rapidly evolving AI-enabled threats, specifically on the growing use of generative AI to stalk and track women, violate the privacy of domestic violence survivors, and create non-consensual porn deepfakes.
A part of a group of women lawmakers urging Speaker Mike Johnson and Dem Leader Hakeem Jeffries to reject any effort to include a broad moratorium on state and local AI regulation in the NDAA.
Chair of the Committee on Education and the Workforce; held a showcase titled "AI in Education" which featured businesses across the country utilizing AI to enhance education for all students.
Ballotpedia survey response: 'The United States government has an important role to play in the development and use of artificial intelligence.'
Introduced Foreign Robocall Elimination Act to strengthen efforts against unlawful robocalls.
Notes that the focus remains on reducing regulatory barriers to ensure that American innovation continues to lead the way

Signed a letter to the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy urging the Administration to incorporate silicon carbide into the AI Action Plan; a part of the effort to reduce AI's growing energy footprint with advanced semiconductor tech
Believes that the U.S. must support AI literacy and technical skills to advance the workforce
Asked Michael Kratsio if the lack of federal preemption is among the top three hurdles to innovation
Has expressed concern about federal overreach in AI's use in education, but has not addressed the preemption question specifically
Chairman of Subcommittee on Communications and Technology; believes AI should receive same light regulatory touch that enabled internet; promotes innovation-friendly approach to AI regulation

Led a letter to the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy urging the Administration to incorporate silicon carbide into the AI Action Plan; a part of the effort to reduce AI's growing energy footprint with advanced semiconductor tech

Acknowledges the capabilities of AI helping warfighters, but asserts that no AI System replaces the judgment, training, and experience of a warfighter; says that a human in the loop isn't a formality but instead a requirement
Argued the proper role of government is to "educate and set conditions, not to actually make decisions" and framed overregulation as the primary threat to U.S. AI leadership

Warned that regulatory burdens on energy permitting are a self-inflicted barrier in the AI race with China; signed a letter to the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy urging the Administration to incorporate silicon carbide into the AI Action Plan; a part of the effort to reduce AI's growing energy footprint with advanced semiconductor tech
Voted to pass the TAKE IT DOWN Act; previously cosponsored the House companion bill

Discussed with experts in the Education and Workforce Committee whether AI programs in schools are truly in the best interest of our students and whether protections must be written regarding AI's use in the workplace

Signed a letter warning an AI moratorium would undermine bipartisan protections for women and girls and prevent states from responding to rapidly evolving AI-enabled threats, specifically on the growing use of generative AI to stalk and track women, violate the privacy of domestic violence survivors, and create non-consensual porn deepfakes
A part of a group of women lawmakers urging Speaker Mike Johnson and Dem Leader Hakeem Jeffries to reject any effort to include a broad moratorium on state and local AI regulation in the NDAA
Says that there's no need for new laws governing AI-generated content used in campaign ads and existing anti-defamation laws should be enough to protect candidates
Co-introduced GUARD Act to establish National Security and Defense AI Institute; supports defense AI readiness and research workforce development
Has introduced many bills regulating AI, including the GUARD Act, the PROACTIV AI Data Act, and the ENFORCE Act
Co-introduced PROACTIV AI Data Act and ENFORCE Act to protect children from AI-generated CSAM; led bipartisan efforts on AI-generated child sexual abuse material penalties
Co-introduced GUARD Act to establish National Security and Defense AI Institute; supports defense AI readiness and research workforce development
Voted in favor of stripping a 10-year moratorium on state AI regulation

Launched aggressive enforcement initiatives under Texas Data Privacy and Security Act (TDPSA); investigations into Character.AI and Meta AI for deceptive trade practices; first-of-its-kind healthcare AI settlement
Investigated Meta and Character.AI for misleading children about AI mental health services; launched investigations under SCOPE Act; leading nation in AI-related children's privacy enforcement
Investigated Meta and Character.AI for misleading children about AI mental health services; launched investigations under SCOPE Act; leading nation in AI-related children's privacy enforcement
Wants to pass legislation to enforce and strengthen export controls to keep advanced AI chips out of the hands of adversaries
Says that we can and must encourage innovation while setting clear guidelines that protect Americans from harm
Asserts that AI data centers should not disrupt communities or raise energy costs for Americans
Wants to pass commonsense safeguards to keep kids safe online, including giving parents more control over their children's social media use
Advocates for investing in STEM education to foster the next eneration of responsible tech leaders; wants to protect workers from invasive AI surveillance in the workplace
In the Texas legislature, protected victims in cases involving explicit deepfake material by requiring social media platforms to create effective reporting and investigation processes
Asserts that AI companies should follow the same copyright laws as everyone else
Writes about AI with neither alarm nor inaction; proactive oversight focused on human control, interpretability, and congressional accountability, without sweeping mandates
Co-introduced bipartisan TRAIN Act with Congresswoman Dean to require AI training data transparency for copyright holders; emphasizes AI innovation should be built on integrity
Pressed questioned State Department Under Secretary Thomas DiNanno about the sale of US chips to China during a House Foreign Affairs Committee hearing

Introduced an amendment to the FY26 National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA) to accelerate the responsible adoption of AI across defense mission areas, including logistics, intelligence, maintenance, and cyber defense

Introduced FAST Act to authorize Defense Department procurement of software/data for AI systems; led introduction of Protecting AI and Cloud Competition in Defense Act; emphasizes AI for military superiority over China
Advocates for free-market principles and reduced regulations in AI/cloud procurement; opposes heavy regulatory approach
Supports Genesis Mission to maintain AI leadership against China; emphasizes reducing reliance on Chinese supply chains in AI research
Backs Trump's Genesis Mission for AI innovation but expresses caution about uncontrolled AI; warns 'we need to be very cautious' and don't want society replaced by AI
Advocates for integrating artificial intelligence (AI) into the U.S. military to maintain an edge over competitors

At a House Small Business Subcommittee on Rural Development, Energy and Supply Chains hearing in January on AI data center infrastructure, said investments in “advanced energy solutions” for data centers “keep us competitive with the AI race with China”
Campaign platform emphasizes cutting burdensome regulations that slow AI innovation; favors light regulatory touch; AI super PAC support indicates pro-deregulation stance
Supports protecting sensitive American data and securing frontier AI R&D facilities as response to China's technological expansion
Advocates for securing frontier AI research and infrastructure as national security priority against China
Supports rigorous safety reviews for powerful AI systems and transparency when AI impersonates humans; opposes nonconsensual deepfakes
Calls for strong protections against nonconsensual deepfakes and digital exploitation of minors
Campaigns on protecting American innovation from China's economic espionage; supports smart investments in American AI infrastructure
Advocates for strengthening U.S. technological superiority in AI against China
Former Elon Musk lawyer and Trump-backed AI super PAC beneficiary ($747K spent); positions favor light regulatory touch; campaigns on fighting policies that protect American AI innovation
Calls for smart investments in AI infrastructure while also protecting rural Texans from projects that drive up electricity rates and drain water supplies
States Congress should regulate AI responsibly; advocates for preventing data centers from draining community resources
Opposes data centers hiking energy costs and draining community resources; supports regulating AI to prevent negative infrastructure impacts
Introduced bipartisan Protecting Families from AI Data Center Energy Costs Act requiring FERC conference on protecting residents from increased costs

Introduced Protecting Families from AI Data Center Energy Costs Act with bipartisan partner Rep. Landsman; chairs hearings on AI data center energy demands; advocates for nuclear and energy expansion to support AI infrastructure while protecting communities
Introduced GUARD Act to establish Military AI Institute at senior military college; emphasizes ensuring U.S. maintains lead over China in AI for defense and national security
Supports military AI development and research initiatives; co-sponsored H.R. 6304 codifying national AI strategy
Very skeptical and critical of how giant technology companies run economy and people's lives with little public oversight
Supports AI deployment in military systems; chairs Energy Subcommittee examining AI infrastructure needs
Advocates for regulated energy approach to support AI infrastructure; co-headlining Data Center and Energy Summit

Major advocate for nuclear energy to power AI data centers; chairs hearings on Powering Demand: Nuclear Solutions for AI; emphasizes nuclear baseload power reliability for AI workloads; supports DOE sites for data center hosting
Advocates for clear rules protecting consumers, supporting innovation, and ensuring ethical AI use; calls for AI strengthening economy without exploitation; emphasizes worker protections
Critical about AI impact on local infrastructure and national security; skeptical of large-scale AI technology infrastructure projects
Views AI as national security concern but not specifically detailed on military applications
Criticized for concerns about data center impact on local infrastructure; skeptical of AI development without proper oversight
Fairly critical about impact of data centers on local infrastructure; skeptical of large AI infrastructure projects
Received significant support from American Mission AI super PAC ($579K in ad spending)
At GAO advised Congress on emerging technologies including AI; contributed to Science & Tech Spotlight on generative AI addressing hallucinations, oversight and privacy concerns
Expressed concern about AI making it hard to determine what's real/fake; no detailed policy positions found
Data center regulation emerged as major campaign platform plank; concerns about AI data center impacts
Supports regulatory actions around AI use by companies to protect workers from job displacement
Believes federal regulation required for AI data centers due to significant water/power/environmental impacts; views as federal issue with multi-state resource implications
Co-introduced Health Tech Investment Act (H.R. 6197) for AI/ML medical device FDA reimbursement pathway
Appointed to House Task Force on Artificial Intelligence by Speaker; task force examined AI impacts on education, health, IP and offered guardrails recommendations
Member of AI Task Force on Financial Services Committee
Supports AI use to reduce red tape and modernize government; emphasizes ethical AI, privacy protections, civil liberties and job safeguards
Criticizes AI boom and lack of meaningful regulation; calls for community protection from data center expansion
Describes Texas data center boom as threat to natural resources; criticizes land grabs, resource seizure, and environmental impact; skeptical of economic benefits
Received support from Leading the Future PAC promoting AI innovation agenda; indicates pro-AI industry alignment
As state legislator established AI Advisory Council for responsible AI use in state agencies
Champions legislation to protect children from harmful social media algorithms and keep AI-generated content safe; supports safeguards against targeted algorithmic content to minors
Co-led bipartisan, bicameral bill to streamline federal permitting for microchip projects; supports export controls on semiconductors in AI competition with China
Stated 'AI should defend democracy, not undermine it' and criticized fully autonomous weapons with no human oversight
AI-focused super PAC (Public First Action/Jobs and Democracy) spent $150K supporting; appears aligned with AI safety/regulation agenda
Endorsed renewable energy investment for data center powering
Science Committee chair overseeing national AI initiatives; supports investments in research/computing recognizing strategic competition
Science Committee oversees military/defense AI research; emphasis on Trump administration AI dominance agenda
House Science Committee championed AI legislation on research, workforce training, model evaluations, and standards
Held committee hearing on federal permitting challenges for data center infrastructure; focus on powering America's AI future
Introduced Stop AI Price Gouging and Wage Fixing Act; legislation addressing AI data center accountability and worker protection
Proposed legislation making AI data centers accountable for costs rather than families bearing expenses
Listed 'creating AI jobs' as policy priority; focus on job creation in AI sector
Emphasizes winning AI race against China; frames AI competition within national security and technology dominance context
Frames AI development within national security context; supports advancing defense systems and US technological leadership
Advocates for AI advancement and leadership; emphasis on innovation rather than regulation
Major platform: support for 'Digital Dome' including AI/quantum/robotics; advocates massive reliable energy production for data centers and defense systems
Co-sponsored Kids Online Safety Act of 2023 (S.1409)
Voted to strike the section relating to AI preemption
Supports innovation but only with strong guardrails
Asserts that Congress needs to pass legislation that protects the public, especially kids
Says Congress should prevent AI from becoming a tool to undercut wages or replace workers without accountability
Argues that Congress should pass enforceable rules for AI generated deepfakes
Established task force that ensures effective coordination in addressing evolving AI challenges
Announces Dept. of Justice grant supporting Mississippi Internet Crimes Against Children Task Force
Involved in upskilling Mississippi, particularly rural Mississippi
Views AI as a tool for innovation but a tool that must be managed with a human-centric approach
Asserts that there should be federal safeguards to ensure AI doesn't lead to mass displacement of workers
Introduced bill that would require DHS to provide notification to Congress to explain all uses or extensions of transaction authority involving AI.

Discussed the need for Congress to secure the "virtual border" at a joint hearing between the Subcommittees on Border Security and Enforcement and Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Protection, which focused on the risks of digital technologies.
Says that AI is a helpful aid to law enforcement, but should not be a substitute for trained personnel.
Argues that if industry is overregulated, adversaries will advance AI capabilities before the US
Says that AI innovation must benefit everyone, not just tech industry giants
Former Lt. Governor supports workforce development and training for AI; advocates pro-worker solutions ensuring workers have a seat at the table in determining how AI is implemented in workplaces
Says that the Government should set clear guardrails and strong standards against deepfake fraud, especially in high risk contexts
Believes in improving export controls on sensitive technologies so China can't buy or steal criticial capabilities

Champions 'balanced, commonsense approach to AI that encourages innovation...strengthens American competitiveness' but opposes overregulation; emphasizes avoiding massive new bureaucracies/regulatory schemes

Says if data centers are going to expand in Illinois, they must pay their fair share for infrastructure and energy demand; opposed to policy that drives up cost; argues that infrastructure needs to grow in order to support innovation, strengthen national security, and compete with China
Says Congress should prevent AI from being used for fraud and surveillance abuse.
Involved in the Congressional Bi-Partisan Artificial Intelligence Caucus to develop smart policy that balances AI's risks and rewards; backed by the pro-tech PAC Leading the Future
Talks about leading high-tech working training efforts in the age of automation, robotics, and AI
Believes that the Government's role is to set clear, responsible guardrails including holding AI platforms to strict data privacy and security standards to protect users
Views AI as a powerful tool that should empower Americans, not replace them; asserts that when used responsibly, AI can improve workplace efficiency and reduce dangerous tasks
Writes that export conrols on advanced AI chips are critical to ensure that AI is developed in America for the benefit of Americans
Asserts that AI has the potential to change our day-to-day lives which means we need strong and thoughtful federal regulation

Believes that organizations must also be required to disclose pertinent information as it relates to data centers, their environment and community impact, and job opportunities before being allowed to open and operate in communities; data centers should provide ample job training for local workers, rather than outsourcing jobs
Argues that there must be strict regulation as it pertains to the safety of kids, teens, and vulnerable populations, ensuring chatbots and other AI tools are built with safety top of mind.
Argues that there must be strict regulation as it pertains to the safety of kids, teens, and vulnerable populations, ensuring chatbots and other AI tools are built with safety top of mind.

Says that AI groups should work hand in hand with labor leaders to ensure the work force and our economy handles AI integration to the benefit of working class Americans, not just corporations and billionaires
Writes that in the absence of a robust federal framework, states should be able to regulate AI based on the needs of their communities
Part of a group of lawmakers that investigated the impact of data centers on energy costs
Cosponsored the AI Ads Act which is a bipartisan bill to combat fraudulent AI campaign ads
Voted no on H.R. 1, One Big Beautiful Bill Act, Cruz Amendment

AI, like all technologies, can be a useful tool for humanity. In the hands of the ruling class, it will be used to cut jobs, monitor consumer behavior, and otherwise serve their priorities, rather than ours.
Upon Meta ending fact checking, Quigley released a statement saying that misinformation and disinformation on social media endangers American's health and safety and the viability of democracy

Co-sponsored the Guaranteeing and Upholding Americans' Right to Decide Responsible AI Laws and Standards (GUARDRAILS) Act which sets to repeat Trump's executive order entitled “Ensuring a National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence” designed to establish a moratorium on state-level artificial intelligence policies
Acknowledges that AI will create huge job growth while reducing repetitive and non-thinking jobs; advocates neutralizing the influence and manipulation of innovators and industries controlling it
A part of the House Committee on Financial Service's Task Force on Artificial Intelligence
Co-sponsored Energy Bills Relief Act requiring data centers to pay fair share of electricity costs rather than passing burden to households and small businesses
Committed to working hard to ensure that America remains a hub of technology innovation, especially in emerging areas like AI
Says that the US can't fall behind countries like China in AI development
Supports AI regulation; emphasizes AI's impact on economy, education, healthcare and national security; advocates ensuring vulnerable communities included in AI future development
Argues that as more data centers companies move to the Midwest, Illinois should upgrade energy grids to attract and retain AI-intensive industries

Advocates for AI literacy and STEM education from early childhood through adult training programs to fill the gap of AI-aligned roles; says that in order to harness the full potential of AI, there must be a comprehensive ecosystem that addresses workforce development
Says that federal and state courts should punish people who intentionally libel or slander by using AI

Pro-AI super PAC Leading the Future spent $1.1 million for Bean according to Chicago Sun Times; asserts that winning the US-China race will come from sustained investment in research, talent, and responsible governance; says the U.S. should lead in setting global standards for AI that protect civil liberties
Suports holding tech companies accountable for harmful practices and strengthening protections for children's privacy and mental health online

Views AI as a tremendous opportunity for innovation, job creation, and solving real world problems; champions a "Digital Dome" legislation framework that secures data privacy, promotes ethical AI development through incentives, and biased algorithms
Mentions AI's impact on energy and water use

Asserts that Congress must develop a policy framework that promotes and champions America’s AI leadership and encourages responsible AI development to create good-paying jobs, protect the environment, keep kids safe, and ensure that AI promotes prosperity for the working class
Advocates for banning addictive design social media legislation for kids, increasing algorithmic transparency; asserts that there should be more research on the effects of social media on society

Says that there need to be basic laws to stop the internet from becoming a tool in interfering elections and misinformation; should require platforms to strengthen safeguards against coordinated disinformation campaigns
Introduced the Investing in Tomorrow's Workforce Act that supports training programs to ensure workers aren't left behind as automation accelerates

Co-sponsored the Guaranteeing and Upholding Americans' Right to Decide Responsible AI Laws and Standards (GUARDRAILS) Act which sets to repeat Trump's executive order entitled “Ensuring a National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence” designed to establish a moratorium on state-level artificial intelligence policies
Key sponsor on the Chip Security Act which would require high-end chip manufacturers to implement technical security measures to detect and prevent smuggling to unauthorized countries and end users

Has been a key sponsor on a number of AI-related legislation including the Chip Security Act and the Responsible and Ethical AL Labeling Act; chairs the House Committee on Financial Service's Task Force on Artificial Intelligence
Argues that "there will be a survival advantage to algorithmic systems that are opaque and deceptive"

Met with OpenAI CEO Sam Altman about universal basic income as a response to AI labor disruption; notes early predictions focused on factory workers and truck drivers; said that AI displacement is no longer a problem limited to blue-collar workers but everyone

Introduced Responsible and Ethical AI Labeling (REAL) Act requiring federal agencies and officers to clearly label any content published via official government channels that is generated or manipulated using AI; says the threat of deepfakes can't be solved with detection; says that the government should provide people ways of defending themselves against deepfakes like a mobile ID tied to a Real ID-compliant credential

Co-sponsored the Guaranteeing and Upholding Americans' Right to Decide Responsible AI Laws and Standards (GUARDRAILS) Act which sets to repeat Trump's executive order entitled “Ensuring a National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence” designed to establish a moratorium on state-level artificial intelligence policies
Acknowledged how quickly the creative arts have come under assault from AI
Advocates for requirements around human-in-the-loop decision making; says AI systems used by the federal government must meet high standards for explainability, data integrity, and security
Believes in a risk-based regulatory approach where there are lighter rules for low-risk applications and stricter scrutiny for high-impact uses
Contends that the federal government should establish clear baseline guidelines, while allowing states to adopt more stringent regulations if they choose

Says data centers can place significant strain on local communities through high energy consumption and environmental impact; believes that data centers should be required to meet federal standards first in addition to complying with state and local laws
Argues that states should be able to adopt more stringent regulations than federal regulations

Wants the Veteran Affairs department "to ensure that human involvement isn't eliminated as a part of the critical nature of the care that we want to be able to provide to a veteran with suicide prevention effort."
Introduced Veteran DATA Act to prevent third-party vendors from monetizing or misusing veterans' data to power AI models

Views AI as means to extend human capabilities, automate routine tasks, and address challenges beyond natural limits; argues that government should have a proactive yet light touch federal approach that harnesses AI's potential without stifling growth
Mentions that a proactive yet light-touch federal approach can harness AI's potential without stifling growth
Wrote letter following Homeland Security Committee hearing examining election-related misinformation and disinformation threats
Says that the foremost protection should be protecting minor children from technology grooming and indoctrinating them
Views AI as both an opportunity and a challenge; says government should focus on protecting workers and consumers
Says Government should require accountability when AI is used in hiring or insurance; advocates for investing in training and workforce development
Asserts that the Government should focus on stopping AI-driven scams and deepfakes

Writes that companies pursuing artificial general intelligence will make the power wielded by Meta look like child's play; says that the government should use the expertise of impartial researchers, scholars, technologists, ethicists, and policymakers to develop policies that establish safeguards and accountability

Introduced the Advanced AI Security Readiness Act to develop an AI security playbook to defend American technology against foreign adversaries; introduced the Chip Security Act preventing the Chinese Communist Party from smuggling American-manufactured AI chips; supports the FY2026 NDAA where one of the priorities were the Advanced AI Security Readiness Act requiring the Director of the National Security Agency to develop guidance to protect artificial intelligence (AI) technology from adversaries by engaging with AI developers and leveraging industry expertise to address vulnerabilities and threats

Introduced No DeepSeek on Government Devices Act to ban the use and download of Deepseek from government devices; granted money to ramp up technology used in real-time data collection for police investigations, including use of AI in conjuction with license plate reading cameras
Supports a private-sector-led, innovation-first approach, pointing to the success of Apple, Microsoft, and Amazon as evidence that "allowing the private sector to flourish" is the right model
Emphasizes that the US needs to win when it comes to AI and quantum computing because its the future of country
Claims that 50 states passing individual laws creates confusing patchworks of regulations; acknowledges that 10 years may be too long
Among a group of lawmakers who sent a letter to the Secretary of Defense Hegseth urging collaboration with Ukraine on the production of AI-enabled, unmanned aerial systems (drones)

Introduced the Quashing Unwanted and Interruptive Electronic Telecommunications (QUIET) Act which requires robocallers to disclose when they use AI and increase penalties for scammers who use AI to impersonate individuals

Takes a strongly pro-AI view which is that the role of government should be to unleash American innovation, not bury it in red tape; says that whoever wins the Ai race will shape the future of global economy, modern warfare and the information space

Quoted in an article: “We still want to be a leader in AI. We don’t want China to surpass us,” Gallagher said. “The trick is really finding the right balance. I think there’s going to be a lot of discussion on this front.”
Supported the CHIPS Act as a vehicle for keeping the U.S. competitive in AI, but he hasn't authored or publicly championed specific AI safety legislation at the federal level

As Ranking Member on Natural Resources, Huffman became one of the most prominent House Democrats opposing the SPEED Act which sought to fast-track permitting for AI data centers and other energy infrastructure by weakening environmental review under NEPA; not opposed to building clean energy infrastructure

Asserts that "unprecedented amounts of money behind Artificial Intelligence and the fact China is also in the race towards Artificial Super Intelligence probably makes it impossible to stop completely."
Argues that Congress must thoughtfully lay the groundwork" for legislation that anticipates AI's impact, and that "AI must work for us, not against us."

Claims that AI could replace 25% of all American jobs right now, warns that "tens of millions of Americans will be driven out of work" in the coming years, and says that "if AI must take away our work, then AI must work for us"; calls for Congress to lay the groundwork for Universal Basic Income (UBI) as a necessary response

Concerned about AI-powered disinformation, writing that "if we Americans cannot develop better ways to recognize what is true, then vicious liars empowered by AI will surely have us at each other's throats."; proposes unusual legislation where politicians who make contradictory public claims can be forced before a judge, with the one found lying facing perjury charges and prison, as a way to combat AI-amplified political lying

Against new construction of any AI data centers until sweeping regulations are passed to prioritize residential water, electricity, and safety; argues companies should have to prove they will not harm people before construction and they should foot the bill for all damages they cause
Says that AI should enhance jobs, not replace them, until we have universal basic income
Supports making AI companies compensate all whose work was stolen without permission to train their models
Introduced legislation requiring Congressional approval before the sale of any advanced AI chip to China
Said Congress didn't ‘put up guardrails’ around social media and that ‘we shouldn't make that same mistake with AI’
Secured $800,000 for the Sacramento County Sheriff’s Office to upgrade forensic technology used to investigate and prevent online child exploitation
Worries "a lot about this epidemic of loneliness" and that AI could enable people to have relationships with artificial beings
Believes that AI will augment rather than fully replace skilled human workers, at least in healthcare.

Asserts that it's the right thing for Congress to have a regulatory framework at the federal level instead of all 50 states; says that Congress should work with states to adopt a framework that could easily be adopted at the federal level
Says that Congress needs representatives who will move legislation rather than just post about it
Argues for a moratorium on new large-scale data center development

Introduced the SAFE AI Research Grants Act to fund safety research through the National Academy of Sciences and develop public ethical guidelines, saying "rapid advancements in AI hold the promise of untold benefits to society, but also present unknown risks" and that understanding and mitigating those risks is "a task of the highest importance and urgency"

As Subcommittee Chairman on Early Childhood, Elementary, and Secondary Education, chaired a series of "Building an AI-Ready America" hearings focused on equipping teachers and students with AI skills; wants to prepare workers rather than protect them from displacement

Introduced the AI Fraud Deterrence Act to increase penalties for using AI to commit wire fraud, mail fraud, bank fraud, or money laundering, saying it would make it "likelier that innovation will serve the public good" by cracking down on criminal misuse

Co-sponsored the STAR Act to extend the 25% advanced manufacturing tax credit for chip production and expand eligibility to certain design expenses, framing it as necessary for the U.S. to "win the chip race" against global competitors
Ranking Member of the House Energy and Commerce Communications and Technology Subcommittee (one of the primary AI jurisdiction panels) and has consistently pushed for consumer-protective guardrails.

Co-introduced the GUARDRAILS Act in March 2026, which would repeal the Trump administration's executive order establishing a national AI policy framework and block efforts to impose a moratorium on state-level AI regulation

Frames Congress's failure to address AI as a specific failure of governance, and criticizes the current administration for focusing AI policy on "promoting right-wing speech in chatbots rather than protecting the American people"

Calls for a UBI funded specifically by "an appropriate tax on profits derived from displacing workers through automation," framing it as a "technology dividend to ensure that all people are benefiting from the emerging technology economy"

Draws a direct parallel to social media, warning Congress not to repeat the same mistake of letting investor returns drive deployment without proportional worker benefit; calls on Congress to "take an active role in shaping how AI is integrated into our lives and in developing thoughtful regulations that balance protecting workers with fostering economic growth"

Warns that AI is already being used "as a weapon in retaliation against workers who are trying to unionize or to suppress wages," that it risks making workplace discrimination harder to prove, and that without worker input it will "exacerbate the ever-widening power gap between workers and employers"; calls for workers and their representatives to "have a seat at the table in the development and implementation of AI in America's workplaces"; cites The AI Con by Alex Hanna and Emily Bender to frame his concern that AI is a vehicle for "a few major well-placed players" to extract value from "other people's creative work, personal data, or labor"
Warning about "extracting value from other people's creative work" suggests sympathy with creator and copyright concerns, but doesn't state a direct legislative position on training data rights.

Frames his concern as "leaving decisions about humanity's future in the hands of a handful of tech CEOs may be the most dangerous form of deregulation yet"; says the regulations that are being proposed are nothing near the scale of the problem that we're facing
Argues for "treating computing power as a utility" and making publicly owned AI infrastructure available rather than allowing AI monopolies to consume an ever-growing share of GDP
Advocates for a federal jobs guarantee, arguing that care work, education, and other AI-resistant sectors have "massive job shortages" and "no training programs"

Said "It is the AI industry claiming that the technology is rapidly developing. I am skeptical about the technology itself and the rate of growth, which might turn out to be a tech bubble; says "AI is really automation and algorithms, maybe on steroids"; proposes regulating AI sector by sector
Regulating AI "not taking away jobs" is a stated guiding principle, but no specific mechanisms.

Working Group Chair of the New Democrat Coalition Workforce & Education Working Group which says to "communicate school AI policies with commonsense guardrails clearly to parents, guardians, and families so they can make informed decisions and understand how these tools may be used in classrooms for their student’s success, including AI literacy, ethical reasoning, and creative problem solving."
Led a letter to the House Appropriations Subcommittee on Labor, Health and Human Services, Education, and Related Agencies, in support of child sexual abuse prevention research activities

Working Group Chair of the New Democrat Coalition Workforce & Education Working Group which says to "integrate AI into U.S. education so American workers are at the forefront of developing and deploying these technologies, as well as prepare to create and fill the new jobs they will generate" and "utilize AI in partnership with government and labor organizations to both better understand the types of jobs and skills that will be in-demand in the future and prepare workers to use new technologies."
Says that "the federal government has to get out of the way" in a Washington Reporter article
Advocates for energy infrastructure to support AI data centers and passed legislation to streamline permitting
Asserts that regulation is needed to help protect children and provide guardrails to protect our economy
Mentions that AI datacenters raise costs and provide limited benefits to the communities they surround on Candidate Website
Asserts that regulation is needed to help protect children on candidate website
"We cannot have Biden's allies in anti-innovation states like California and Massachusetts  setting the trend on over-regulating AI"

Argues that we need a national framework instead of a patchwork of policies'; says that "We need a national framework; we don’t want a patchwork of policies on AI, especially having states like California set the agenda" in the Washington Reporter

Co-sponsored the AI OVERWATCH Act to ensure U.S. adversaries cannot use American AI technology to further military and intelligence programs, curb the sale of chips to our adversaries such as China, and accelerate American AI exports to allies and partners around the world; "
Introduced multiple bills including the AI OVERWATCH Act and the Industries of the Future Act

Wrote a post saying that HB 1333 (a bill restricting local control on the development of data centers) is bad policy, but did not further comment; said that he "knows the value of data centers" and that "it's going to increase the energy needs, and so I have great faith in our local people to take care of that"

Introduced the Tech Diplomacy Training Act to ensure all Foreign Service officers receive training on STEM concepts prior to being stationed abroad to be "ready"; introduced the Industries of the Future Act which would increase capacity for research programs of federal govt. that focus on industries like AI

Said AI is "something that we're trying to keep a rein on... but the problem with AI is that we're holding onto the tail" and "it's just advancing so quick - we pass some, but by the time we pass it ,we need to pass more"
Co-authored House Bill 1333 which restricted local regulation and control on the development of data centers
Talked about children being extorted on a Fox 59 channel, but no clear policy position has made

After Haggard's wife was targeted in AI deepfake, Haggard said that he would fight to let people know that this is a serious thing and we need to continue to fight to protect folks; introduced a bill that would modify state law to match the federal 'Take It Down Act' and makes the penalties for creating and distributing AI porn in Indiana similar to the penalties at the federal level — up to three years in prison and fines

Haggard explicitly said he doesn't like the threat of pulling funding from states with AI laws, and anticipates Indiana's state-level protections will remain on the books, though he doesn't state it as a hard policy position
Concerned about AI and automation's labor market impacts not properly addressed by policies

Released a news clip on social media with "I am grateful to serve on this committee with fellow legislators and experts from our state who are committed to understanding this emerging technology so that Indiana can regulate it to strike a needed balance" in the caption

Authored a bill SB 135 that requires data centers to submit reports to Indiana that incldues how much water and electricity each one used; says that "with a nearly 30% increase in data centers since March, we need to have proper checks in place for both grid and water supply integrity moving forward"

Says that "our responsibility is clear: protect kids, empower parents and hold social media companies accountable when they fail to safeguard young users. Indiana families deserve better. I will continue working with my colleagues to ensure our laws put the safety and well-being of our children first" in a social media statement
A State Senator part of the AI Task Force which discussed the impact of AI on the workforce and manufacturing
A State Senator part of the AI Task Force which discussed AI's impact on copyrighting
Says on candidate website that with increasing automation threatening traditional jobs, UBI provides a solution by decoupling income from employment, ensuring no one is left behind"
Explicitly calls for government regulation, standards, and oversight of powerful technologies including AI on Candidate Website under priorities
References scammers using false information amplified by algorithms and Americans being enticed to give up savings based on false information

Introduced the Strengthening Export Controls Compliance Act to help American businesses navigate increasingly complex export control rules and stay globally competitive; introduced the BIS Strengh Act to strengthen staffing at the Department of Commerce’s Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS), which administers and enforces U.S. export controls on sensitive and dual-use technologies; said "Keeping our most advanced AI chips out of Chinese hands is crucial for both national security and economic strength"
Says that companies behind AI data centers are getting incentives and tax breaks while families are dealing with utility costs; asserts investing in green technology will help make a difference
Participated in the Select Committee hearing on China's campaign to steal America's AI edge; warned about China acquiring frontier AI capabilities through both legal and illegal channels

Asserts that if American models keep its lead over China, the American people will be able to decide the rules of the road and enact protections for workers against displacement, ensure communities do not face hikes to their utility bills, and ensure civil rights and human rights are designed into the algorithm in a committee hearing on China’s Campaign to Steal America’s AI Edge
Raised community concerns about data centers raising electricity prices but also acknowledged the need for compute infrastructure to win the AI race against China
Asserts that if American models keep its lead over China, the American people will be able to decide the rules of the road and enact protections for workers against displacement

Calls for mandatory pre-deployment safety evaluations, independent third-party audits, registration of large-scale training runs, whistleblower protections, and a Technical Advisory Corps inside Congress

Warns against permanent infrastructure monopolies controlled by a handful of firms and calls for antitrust enforcement and expanded public access to compute infrastructure; opposes concentrated private control of AI infrastructure
Proposes a federal AI Workforce Transition Fund, wage insurance, mid-career retraining programs, paid reskilling pathways, and small-business AI adoption grants
Calls for transparency standards for generative systems and election-cycle risk protocols to combat synthetic media distorting public discourse

Says that there should be responsible regulation that doesn't stifle innovation; a part of the Working Group on Artificial Intelligence through the U.S. House of Representatives Financial Services Committee where she says "Innovation in AI presents opportunities for economic growth, job creation, and increased efficiency in our local financial institutions, but we must also be alert to the potential dangers and need for guardrails. I look forward to working with my colleagues on this effort to ensure that the U.S. is well-equipped to use this transformative technology"; hopeful that the House and Senate will come together on AI legislation

Aims to raise standard social media access age to 16; introduced the AI Warnings And Resources for Education (AWARE) Act, the first House legislation to address the growing use of AI chatbot companions by minors; introduced the Safeguarding Adolescents From Exploitative BOTs (SAFEBOTs) Act which requires chatbot providers to clearly disclose to minors when they are interacting with an artificial intelligence system and not a real person; says that "Protecting kids online is one of the most urgent challenges we face in today's digital age"; introduced the RESET Act which requires platforms to default minors into safer settings, limit addictive and harmful algorithmic content, and give parents stronger oversight tools; co-led the Parents Over Platforms Act which closes the loophole that allows kids to bypass age limits by requiring app stores and developers to share responsibility for age assurance and blocking high-risk content for minors

Asserts that there should be mandated and clear warnings for AI chatbots that simulate human relationships or encourage self-harm; introduced the AI Warnings And Resources for Education (AWARE) Act, the first House legislation to address the growing use of AI chatbot companions by minors; introduced the Safeguarding Adolescents From Exploitative BOTs (SAFEBOTs) Act which requires chatbot providers to clearly disclose to minors when they are interacting with an artificial intelligence system and not a real person

Said "When we talk about preemption, I would want states to be able to go farther,” to The Daily Signal, “so I would prefer to see a floor rather than a ceiling, for their ability to innovate and respond to kids online safety concerns, because the states are going to be way more nimble at addressing concerns from parents than the federal government.”

Says to "create a government agency to demand access to all social media algorithms operating in the United States, and pass safety measures to combat addiction, division, disinformation, and youth mental health decline" on candidate website

Introduced ADVERSARIES Act to close export control to strengthen U.S. export controls and prevent foreign adversaries like the Chinese Communist Party from exploiting loopholes to access sensitive U.S. technology

States that we must address data centers' environmental impacts because these facilities consume massive amounts of energy and water; supports local communities' right to reject data centers that threaten their environment and infrastructure; asserts that we must strengthen EPA oversight to require comprehensive environmental impact studies; asserts that we should end members of Congress trading in tech companies building these facilities

Says that he will provide specialized training and support for workers displaced by AI and technological change and prepare the next generation for the jobs of tomorrow; asserts that 2025 was one of the worst job markets for new college graduates in years as technology changes and AI displacement make it even harder for young people to get started

Asserts that instead of "banning AI data centers in Ohio, let’s require them to power their own operations and work with us to modernize the grid, making power more reliable, sustainable, and affordable for everyone"
Advocates for a complete data center moratorim because there are no long term studies of it and because of the increase in utilities
Wishes for programs for the new growth industry of autonomous vehicles, robotics, and AI
Spoke on a podcast on how it's important to put parameters around AI in a way that will protect people, environment, democracy, and free speech; asserts that we are a little bit behind
